standard_description: Interpret whole-number quotients of whole numbers, e.g., interpret 56 ÷ 8 as the number of objects in each share when 56 objects are partitioned equally into 8 shares, or as a number of shares when 56 objects are partitioned into equal shares of 8 objects each.
sources: Carpenter, T.P., Fennema, E., Franke, M.L., Levi, L., & Empson, S.B. (2014). Children's Mathematics: Cognitively Guided Instruction (2nd ed.). Heinemann.
NCTM Progressions for the Common Core State Standards in Mathematics — Operations and Algebraic Thinking (OA) K–5, 2011
Van de Walle, J.A., Karp, K.S., & Bay-Williams, J.M. (2013). Elementary and Middle School Mathematics (8th ed.). Pearson.
EngageNY Grade 3 Module 1 Topic D — Division as Sharing and Measurement, 2013
Smarter Balanced Assessment Consortium — Grade 3 Mathematics Item Specifications, 2015

misconceptions.short_label: Subtracts the two numbers instead of dividing
misconceptions.description: Student treats a division situation as a subtraction problem. For 18 ÷ 6, student computes 18 − 6 = 12 instead of 18 ÷ 6 = 3. Confuses 'taking away once' with 'taking away repeatedly until zero.'
misconceptions.citation: Carpenter et al. (2014) p.93 — 'Subtraction-for-division substitution is the most common operation error in early division work; students apply the more familiar additive operation when the multiplicative situation is unfamiliar.'
misconceptions.remediation: Show repeated subtraction explicitly: 18 − 6 − 6 − 6 = 0 (subtracted three 6's → 18 ÷ 6 = 3). Division is repeated subtraction to zero, not single subtraction.

misconceptions.short_label: Confuses 'how many in each group' vs 'how many groups'
misconceptions.description: Student knows 24 ÷ 4 = 6 but cannot distinguish whether 6 means 'each group has 6' (partitive) or 'there are 6 groups of 4' (quotative). Picks wrong answer in word problems requiring this distinction.
misconceptions.example: '15 cookies split among 5 children' (partitive → 3 each) vs '15 cookies in bags of 5' (quotative → 3 bags). Student gives '5' or confuses the two situations.
misconceptions.citation: EngageNY G3 Module 1 Topic D Teacher Edition — 'The two division models — partitive (sharing) and quotative (measurement) — must both be taught explicitly; students who learn only one cannot interpret word problems flexibly.'
misconceptions.remediation: Always identify before computing: 'Am I finding the SIZE of each group, or the NUMBER of groups?' Draw the situation. Sharing = partitive (deal cards). Measuring = quotative (cut a ribbon into 5-cm pieces).

misconceptions.short_label: Divides smaller by larger by mistake
misconceptions.description: Student writes the division in the wrong direction. Sees 24 ÷ 6 and writes 6 ÷ 24, thinking division is commutative like multiplication. Or writes 18 ÷ 3 in word problem as 3 ÷ 18.
misconceptions.example: 'Share 24 marbles among 6 children' → student writes 6 ÷ 24 (or 'cannot do' if zero remainder is unfamiliar). Correct: 24 ÷ 6 = 4.
misconceptions.citation: Van de Walle et al. (2013) p.171 — 'Division is non-commutative — order matters absolutely. Students who overgeneralize multiplicative commutativity make this error frequently in early division.'
misconceptions.source: Van de Walle Elementary and Middle School Mathematics
misconceptions.remediation: Always write the WHOLE (total amount) first: 'Total ÷ groups = each' or 'Total ÷ each = groups.' Make 'total goes first' a non-negotiable rule. Practice rewriting word problems with the total identified.

misconceptions.description: Student distributes objects without ensuring equal groups. For '24 ÷ 6,' student might draw 6 groups of varying sizes (e.g., 5, 4, 5, 3, 4, 3 = 24) and pick one group's count as the answer.
misconceptions.example: Word problem '20 apples shared equally among 5.' Student draws 5+5+4+3+3 (sums to 20) and answers '5' or 'varies.' Correct: 4 each.
misconceptions.citation: EngageNY G3 Module 1 Topic D Teacher Edition — 'Equal-share enforcement is the conceptual prerequisite for division; students who don't insist on equal groups produce arithmetically valid but division-invalid solutions.'
misconceptions.remediation: Use one-by-one dealing: distribute objects round-robin until exhausted. Verify all groups same size BEFORE counting any single group. Refuse 'unequal' answers.

misconceptions.description: Student knows division as repeated skip-counting (5, 10, 15, 20 → 4 jumps for 20÷5) but loses track of the count. Especially common for ×6, ×7, ×8, ×9 division facts.
misconceptions.example: 56 ÷ 8: student skip counts 8, 16, 24, 32, 40, 48, 56 and reports 8 jumps (correct: 7).
misconceptions.citation: Carpenter et al. (2014) p.95 — 'Skip-count strategies fail at the same factors as in missing-factor problems; finger tracking is the universal fix until division facts become fluent.'
misconceptions.remediation: Use finger tracking: one finger per skip. Number of fingers = quotient. Practice ×6/×7/×8 multiplication facts to fluency to avoid this entirely.

misconceptions.short_label: Doesn't use multiplication to solve division
misconceptions.description: Student treats division as a separate operation from multiplication. For 56 ÷ 8, doesn't think 'what × 8 = 56?' (= 7). Uses inefficient strategies (drawing, skip-counting) instead of the inverse.
misconceptions.example: Student knows 7 × 8 = 56 fluently. Asked 56 ÷ 8, draws 56 dots and partitions into 8 groups laboriously. Misses the direct connection.
misconceptions.citation: NCTM OA Progressions K-5 (2011) p.27 — 'Division as the unknown-factor problem is a Grade 3 cornerstone; students who do not internalize this connection rely on slow strategies that fail under time pressure or for harder facts.'
misconceptions.remediation: For every division fact, ask: 'What multiplication helps?' Build fact-family triangles connecting × and ÷. 56 ÷ 8 = ? → 8 × ? = 56 → 7.

misconceptions.description: Student over-applies a keyword strategy: assumes 'each/every' always signals division, missing context that requires multiplication. Or vice versa: misses 'each' as a division cue when it is the right operation.
misconceptions.example: 'There are 6 boxes with 4 apples in each box. How many apples total?' Student divides 6 ÷ 4 (wrong — multiply 6 × 4 = 24).
misconceptions.citation: Van de Walle et al. (2013) p.173 — 'Keyword-based strategies are unreliable for division word problems; structural understanding (whole-into-equal-parts vs equal-parts-into-whole) is the only robust approach.'
misconceptions.source: Van de Walle Elementary and Middle School Mathematics
misconceptions.remediation: Replace keywords with structural questions: 'Do I know the WHOLE and need PARTS?' (divide) or 'Do I know the PARTS and need the WHOLE?' (multiply). Draw a bar model first, then choose operation.
